coredata.dc:title: Coupling of hydrophobic graphene quantum dots with photochromic molecule for fabrication of transparent photo-responsive polymeric films manifesting FRET functioning
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Razaghi, Masoumeh
Khorasani, Mojtaba
Mohamadnia, Zahra
Kazemi, Foad
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel sensor on the basis of polymeric film comprising hydrophobic graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Amine) as fluorescent donor and spiropyran (SP) as photochromic acceptor was developed. The polymeric fluorescence sensor was synthesized through a four-step approach, beginning with the synthesize of photochromic component (SP) having hydroxyl functional group. Then, the amine-functionalized GQDs denoted as GQDs-Amine were synthesized through direct carbonization of citric acid in the presence of benzyl amine. GQDs-Amine was then decorated with SP to achieve GQDs-Amine-SP. In the final step, by using either polylactic acid (PLA) or polymethyl meta acrylate (PMMA) as polymeric precursors and via a simple solvent casting methodology, the related polymeric films comprising photoactive elements were prepared. All synthesized compounds were characterized using various methods such as nuclearmagnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, elemental analysis (CHN), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). According to the related experiments, 0.582 mmol SP was successfully immobilized per gram of GQDs-Amine-SP. Also, after grafting of SP onto the surface of GQDs-Amine, the contact angle remarkably increased from 19° to 64° due to the difference hydrophobic nature between SP and its merocyanine (MC) form. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomenon occurred via exposure of GQDs-Amine-SP to alternating cycles of UV–vis irradiations. To confirm the FRET phenomenon and existing a good matching between the UV–vis absorption region of SP and the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-Amine, the photoluminescence activity of material was also performed in different mediums as well as in solid state. These solutions were exposed to UV light(365 nm) at various time intervals and their fluorescence properties were investigated under excitation at 400 nm. With increasing the time of UV light exposure up to 220 s, the fluorescence intensity increased at 620 nm, and inversely, the fluorescence intensity decreased at 471 nm. A similar pattern but with higher intensity were also observed for the polymeric films fabricated using phase inversion method. According to the UV–vis and fluorometric analyses, the GQDs-Amine-SP and the corresponding films showed excellent photoreversibility, photostability, and prominentfatigue resistance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spiropyran
Optical Sensor
FRET
Photochromic
Fluorescence
Hydrophobic Graphene Quantum Dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of polymeric films containing GQDs-Amine-SP
Preparation and characterization of GQDs, GQDs-Amine, and GQDs-Amine-SP
The optical behavior of GQDs and their derivatives
Characterization of the transparent photo-responsive films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of GQDs-Amine, SP, and GQDs-Amine-SP in CDCl3 solvent.

Dispersion of GQDs and GQDs-Amine in different solvents (a). Wettability change of disks prepared of the GQDs-Amine and (b), GQDs-Amine-SP (c), and FE-SEM images of the GQDs-Amine and (d-f), GQDs-Amine-SP (g-i).
FESEM, elemental mapping, and CHN analysis of GQDs-Amine (a, c) and GQDs-Amine-SP (b, d).
FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs-Amine, SP, and GQDs-Amine-SP.

(a) UV–vis spectra of SP, GQDs, GQDs-Amine, and GQDs-Amine-SP in dichloromethane solvent, (b) absorption spectra of GQDs-Amine-SP after UV exposure (365 nm) (dashed lines) and after irradiationwith visible light (565 nm) (solid line).
UV–vis and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs in water (a), and GQDs-Amine in dichloromethane (b) at different excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-Amine-SP in dichloromethane upon irradiation with (a) UV light (365 nm) or (b) visible light (565 nm), fluorescence spectra of GQDs-Amine-SP in water–ethanol upon irradiation with (c) UV light (365 nm) or (d) visible light (470 nm) at different irradiation times. (Excitation wavelength of 400 nm was selected).
Overlap of the fluorescence emission spectrum of the GQDs-Amine as the donor and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the MC as the acceptor at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm.

Changes in the FL intensity at 471 nm of GQDs-Amine-SP at different UV irradiation times (365 nm) in dichloromethane (a) and water–ethanol (b). Changes in the FL intensity at 471 nm of GQDs-Amine-SP with visible irradiation time (565 nm) in dichloromethane (c) and water–ethanol (d), fluorescence intensity switching cycle of the GQDs-Amine-SP under alternate UV and visible light irradiation (e). (Excitation wavelength is 400 nm).

Water contact angles of (a) PMMA film, (b) PMMA/GQDs-Amine-SP film, (c) PMMA/GQDs-Amine-SP film after UV light exposure, (d) PLA film, (e) PLA/GQDs-Amine-SP film, and (f) PLA/GQDs-Amine-SP after UV light exposure for 2 min.
Solid-state UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) PMMA/GQDs-Amine-SP and (b) PLA/GQDs-Amine-SP polymeric films under UV light exposure (365 nm) for different times.
Schematic for the preparation of GQDs-Amine-SP as photochromic molecule through the reaction of GQDs-Amine and SP.
Schematic representation of fluorescence modulation of the GQDs-Amine-SP under UV and visible lights.

head.ce:title: Coupling of hydrophobic graphene quantum dots with photochromic molecule for fabrication of transparent photo-responsive polymeric films manifesting FRET functioning

Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences (IASBS)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences (IASBS)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid-state UV–Vis absorption spectra of PMMA/GQDs-Amine-SP.

In this study, a novel sensor on the basis of polymeric film comprising hydrophobic graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Amine) as fluorescent donor and spiropyran (SP) as photochromic acceptor was developed. The polymeric fluorescence sensor was synthesized through a four-step approach, beginning with the synthesize of photochromic component (SP) having hydroxyl functional group. Then, the amine-functionalized GQDs denoted as GQDs-Amine were synthesized through direct carbonization of citric acid in the presence of benzyl amine. GQDs-Amine was then decorated with SP to achieve GQDs-Amine-SP. In the final step, by using either polylactic acid (PLA) or polymethyl meta acrylate (PMMA) as polymeric precursors and via a simple solvent casting methodology, the related polymeric films comprising photoactive elements were prepared. All synthesized compounds were characterized using various methods such as nuclearmagnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, elemental analysis (CHN), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). According to the related experiments, 0.582 mmol SP was successfully immobilized per gram of GQDs-Amine-SP. Also, after grafting of SP onto the surface of GQDs-Amine, the contact angle remarkably increased from 19° to 64° due to the difference hydrophobic nature between SP and its merocyanine (MC) form. The fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomenon occurred via exposure of GQDs-Amine-SP to alternating cycles of UV–vis irradiations. To confirm the FRET phenomenon and existing a good matching between the UV–vis absorption region of SP and the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-Amine, the photoluminescence activity of material was also performed in different mediums as well as in solid state. These solutions were exposed to UV light(365 nm) at various time intervals and their fluorescence properties were investigated under excitation at 400 nm. With increasing the time of UV light exposure up to 220 s, the fluorescence intensity increased at 620 nm, and inversely, the fluorescence intensity decreased at 471 nm. A similar pattern but with higher intensity were also observed for the polymeric films fabricated using phase inversion method. According to the UV–vis and fluorometric analyses, the GQDs-Amine-SP and the corresponding films showed excellent photoreversibility, photostability, and prominentfatigue resistance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are composed of tiny graphene fragments which have lateral size of less than 100 nm and two-dimensional structure. [1–3] Optimal GQDs are frequently comprised of a single atomic layer of carbon. The most common types of GQDs have several atomic layers less than 10 nm in size and contain oxygen and hydrogen atoms. [4] Generally, there are various oxygen-containing functional groups such as epoxy, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups in the structure of GQDs. [5,6] Nowadays, various types of research have been developed to control functional properties through surface chemical modification of GQDs. [7–9] Fluorescence is one of the prominent properties of GQDs. The mechanism of photoluminescence (PL) of graphene quantum dots can be explained by doping atoms, surface functional groups, the size of GQDs, and there is no agreementfor themechanismof PL. The general photoluminescence mechanism of GQDs is the result of two features including the surface/edge state in GQDs and quantum confinement of the conjugated π-domains, and the synergistic effect of these two factors. [10] GQDs usually show fluorescence of red, green, blue, [11] yellow, [12,13] and ultraviolet (UV), [14] which occurs due to various sizes, excitation wavelengths, and functional groups. GQDs prepared via different methods have various UV absorption characteristics, typically showing an absorption peak between 200 and 260 nm attributed to transitions of π to π* and an absorption peak greater than 260 nm related to transitions of n to π*. [13,15] UV–visible absorption is because of the size of GQDs, functionalgroups containing oxygen, [16] and their quantum limitation. [17,18] GQDs havepotential in various biomedicalapplications [19] compared to graphene or graphene oxide (GO) due to their small two-dimensional (2D) size, [20] biocompatibility, and non-toxicity. Two usual procedures were employed to prepare GQDs including “bottom-up” and “top-down”. [21] Despite the widespread development of GQDs, there are still limited reports about the modification of GQDs with various functional groups. The functionalization of GQDs changes their photoluminescence properties, biocompatibility, and electronic properties, reduces non-radiative processes, and conversely, increases their optical properties and quantum efficiency. [22,23] Among various photochromic compounds, spiropyrans (SP) have a special role because changing the structural form of spiropyran to merocyanine, not only changes the non-planar structure of the photochromic substance into a planar form, but also causes a significant increase in the polarity of the molecule. Therefore, it is not surprising that spiropyrans are not only used in organic materials and polymers, but also the ability to couple with biological compounds such as amino acids, peptides, proteins and nucleic acids, and by the binding of spiropyran to the material, the property of changing optical form is also given to the molecules. [24] As a result, spiropyran is more than a simple photochromic. The range of stimuli that lead to stimulation and isomerization is very wide and includes various solvents, metal ions, acids and bases, temperature, reduction potential and mechanical force. Also, the photochromic compound of spiropyran is a well-known molecule due to its tunability, stability, and fast response time. [25] Spiropyran compounds as photochromic molecules can be employed for modification of GQDs. Derivatives of SP can respond to different types of external stimuli, especially light. [26] SP photochromic molecules have various applications [27] in biomedicine, [26] UV/heat detection, memory storage, bio-molecular processing, high-securityanti-counterfeitingink, [28–30] optical memory, [31] acid sensing, etc. due to their high fatigue-resistance and outstanding optical stability. [32] The structure of SP molecules changes reversibly in the presence of external stimuli. These changes can affect their color, chemical, and physical properties. [33,34] Under UV light, the non-planar and colourless SP molecules convert to the highly conjugated and planar isomer of merocyanine (MC) which has an absorption band with high intensity in the visible region due to the spiro carbon–oxygen bond cleavage. This isomer can return to its original form under visible light (green or white) or heat. [26,35,36] In 2013, Li et al. fabricated nanocomposites containing SP with tert-butyldimethylsilyloxy as side group and graphene oxide (GO). The resulting nanocomposite was used for the fluoride (F-) detection and demonstrated sensitive and rapid detection of F- ions in the biological media. Also, the presence of GO in the synthesized nanocomposite accelerated the reaction rate between F− and SP. [37] In 2013, Nahain et al. prepared the fluorescent nanoparticles containing reduced graphene oxide (rGO) modified with dopamine and spiropyran (SP) attached to the hyaluronic acid (HA). The presence of HA in the structure of photoresponsive nanoparticles leads to binding to CD44 cell receptors. The obtained rGO/HA-SP revealed purple color after UV light irradiation (365 nm) due to spiropyranconversion to merocyanine (MC). Accumulation of rGO/HA-SP nanoparticles in cancerous tissues presented the efficient release of graphene in the target tissue. The drug delivery from the rGO/HA-SP surface, showed that these materials act as a drug carrier in the drug release system as well as a fluorescent probe for diagnosis. [38,39] In 2014, Sharker et al. designed the stimuli-responsive fluorescent nanomaterials based on GO attached to a polymer coupled with SP and boron-dipyrromethane as a hydrophobic dye for multicolour targeted bioimaging applications. Fluorescence emission and in vivo cell imaging showed that the obtained nanoparticles with adjustable colors are suitable for imaging applications, especially in the diagnosis of cancer cells. [40] In 2016, Zhao et al. developed a two-photon and dual-emission GQD420 probe for monitoring of hydrogen peroxide in biological systems and deep tissues (0–600 μm) of murine mode.A merocyanine functionalized with boronate ester (BMC) was used as a dual-emission fluorescence modulator and the target-activated trigger for specific detection of hydrogen peroxide and lighting the fluorescence of TPGQD420. The TPGQD420–BMC displayed a rapid fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) response with an emission band from green to blue toward H2O2·H2O2 in the living systems reacts with BMC, causing structural changes and transformationofMCtoSP. Next, according to the FRET phenomenon, the blue luminescence of the TPGQD420 sample returns to its original state. [41] The FRET phenomenon is an interplay between the donor and the acceptor which leads to the transition of the excited energy. This process is influenced by various factors such as the distance between donor and acceptor, the spectral overlap of donoremission and acceptor absorption, the dipole moment of molecules, and so on. FRET has been widely used between ring-opened forms of MC and fluorescent molecules due to the molecular absorptionpeakof theMC in the visible region. [42–44] Effective FRET occurs when the absorption spectrum of the MC overlaps with the emission spectra of the fluorescent molecules at a suitable distance from the environment and in the appropriate molecular direction.

In some reports, SP is non-covalently immobilized on the surface of different nanostructures, which may have disadvantages such as dye leakage and increased dye accumulation. For this reason, a simple bottom-up procedure was employed for the selective preparation of GQDs-Amine by adjusting the degree of carbonization of citric acid (CA) as the starting material. Then, GQDs-Amine-SP was prepared by covalent bonding of spiropyran (SP) to the surface of GQDs-Amine. The fluorescence and photochromic properties of the fabricated materials were investigated. Then, transparent polymeric films containing GQDs decorated with spiropyran (GQDs-Amine-SP) were prepared. GQDs-Amine-SP was ultrasonically dispersed within the polylactic acid (PLA) and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) matrix. The effect of polymer environment on the photochromic and fluorescence properties of the prepared films was investigated. Films exhibited reversible fluorescence modulation, excellent photochromic property, and high stability. The photoresponsive films containing GQDs-Amine-SP can be used to qualitative detection of ultraviolet (UV) lights present in the atmosphere as well as in the light-related devices and so on. According to our knowledge, this system has not been reported yet.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), benzylamine, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), ethanol, and dichloromethane (DCM) were acquired from Merck Company. Piperidine was obtained from Acros Organics™. 2-hydroxy-5-nitrobenzaldehyde, N,N-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 2-bromoethanol, and 2,3,3-Trimethylindolenine were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Industrial grade of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was purchased from Tabriz Petrochemical Company and purified before use. Polylactic acid (PLA) is obtained from Minnetonka company. Tetrahydrofuran (THF) was purchased from Daejung Company. All usedorganicsolvents were driedwith several differentdryingagents.

2-Bromoethanol (22.80 mmol, 1.61 mL) was added to the solution of 2,3,3-trimethylindolenine (18.69 mmol, 3 mL) in dry toluene (30 mL) [45] and the reaction mixture wasrefluxed for 24 h. Then the mixture was placed at ambient temperature and the resulting solid product was washed with cold toluene to obtain the Bromide salt (melting point: 197 ℃, 284.192 g.mol−1, 65.59 %). The light pink product was analyzed using various spectroscopic techniques (Figs. S1-S3). [46].

Benzaldehyde (20.4 mmol, 3.41 g) and piperidine (14.67 mmol, 1.45 mL) were added to the Bromide salt (13.37 mmol, 3.8 g) solution in 40 mL ethanol and the mixture was refluxed for 6 h. The resulting purple solution was stirred at ambient temperature for another hour. The obtained product was separated at 0 °C, washed with cold ethanol to give pure spiropyran (melting point: 168 ℃, 352.38 g.mol−1, 48.3 %), as violet powder, and analyzed using different spectroscopic techniques (Figs. S4-S6). [46].

Carbonization of citric acid (CA) was employed as carbon source for the fabrication of GQDs. [21] In a typical method, CA (19 mmol, 4 g) was heated to 200 °C. CA liquefied in 5 min, then the color of liquid altered to pale yellow, and then to brown during 25 min indicating the formation of GQDs. The obtained product was analyzed using 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopies (Fig. S7).

Citric acid (CA) pyrolysisin the presence of benzylamine was also used for the synthesis of benzylamine functionalized-GQDs (GQDs-Amine). CA (19 mmol, 4 g) was heated at 200 °C in the 5 mL round-bottom flask. Citric acid was liquidated and its color turned from light yellow to light brown within 15 min. Then, benzylamine (9.33 mmol, 1 g) was added dropwise to the GQDs over 10 min. The mixturewas cooledtoambienttemperature and the obtained dark brown solid was washed several times with distilled water and centrifuged at high speed 6000 rpm for 10 min to separate unreacted materials (two phases formed, the liquid phase was discarded and the solid phase was washed several times with distilled water and centrifuged again). Finally, the obtained powder was dissolved in acetone solvent and after evaporation of the solvent, the resulting solid was washed several times with n-hexane solvent to remove unreacted benzylamine. The obtained powder was dried and named as GQDs-Amine. This synthetic approach gave an overall yield of 18 %. The solution of GQDs-Amine showed blue-green luminescence under irradiation at 365 nm.

Under inert and dark atmosphere, GQDs-Amine)0.2 g (, SP (0.567 mmol, 0.2 g), NHS (0.177 mmol, 0.062 g), and DCC (0.566 mmol, 0.117 g) were dissolved in 10 mL of THF and stirred at 40 °C. After 30 h, the mixture was allowed to cool and centrifuged at 6000 rpm. The solution was separated from solid and the solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure. The precipitate was washed with cold ethanol and then with deionized water to separate the unreacted SP, NHS, and other impurities. Finally, the solid was dried at 40 °C under vacuum. This synthetic approach gave an overall yield of 70 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of polymeric films containing GQDs-Amine-SP
ce:section.ce:para: Two polymeric films were fabricated according to the following general procedure. Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) (0.4 g) was added to DCM (20 mL) and the mixture was stirred until completely dissolution. Then GQDs-Amine-SP (10 mg) was added, the polymer solution was stirred for 2 h, and the mixture was placed under 30 w ultrasonic waves for 15 min. Then, the solution was poured into a petri dish and stored in a dark room. After the complete evaporation of the solvent, the obtained thin film under the name of PMMA/GQDs-Amine-SP was peeled off. For preparation of the second film, PVP (0.05 g) and GQDs-Amine-SP (8 mg) were added to DCM solvent (5 mL) and stirred for a few minutes. Then, polylactic acid (PLA) (0.95 g) was added to the solution and the mixture was stirred for 2 h at 25 °C. The prepared homogeneous solution was slowly stirred for 4 h to remove bubbles and the PLA/GQDs-Amine-SP film was prepared according to the above-mentioned method.

The absorption spectra were obtained with a Pharmacia Biotech Ultrospec 4000 UV–vis spectrophotometer. FTIRabsorbancespectra were recorded using a FTIR spectrophotometerBruker Vector22 (Germany). TESCAN MIRA3 instrument was used for filed-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) analysis. The melting points of products were determined by Thermo Scientific type 9200. Elemental analysis was performed using an elemental analyzer (Isoprime 100). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using NETZCH STA 409 PC/PG with a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of the synthesized materials were recorded with a Bruker 400 spectrometer in common organic solvents such as DMSO‑D6, CDCl3, or D2O. Fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) of the synthesized compounds were measured utilizing a Cary Eclipse FL spectrophotometer. Thefluorescencebehavior of thepreparedcompounds was evaluated at different excitation wavelengths before and after irradiation with 365 nm ultraviolet radiation. Fluorescencespectra of polymeric films were recorded by an AvaSpec-128 Starline Ultrafast Fiber-optic Spectrometer. VarianCary5EUV–vis–NIRspectrophotometerwas employed to record UV–vis absorption spectra. The optical properties of the fabricated films were investigated using solid-state UV–vis spectroscopy. The excitation was done by a UV lamp (365 nm, 6 Watt), CAMAG (50/60 Hz) to estimate photochromic properties. A 20 W LED lamp with white light was the visible light source. In order to properly disperse the particles in the polymer solution, the ultrasonic probe QSONICA model Q700 was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of GQDs, GQDs-Amine, and GQDs-Amine-SP
ce:section.ce:para: Spiropyran was synthesized according to Scheme 1 through the nucleophilic substitution between 2,3,3-trimethyl indoline and bromoethanol followed by a condensation reaction between the resulting Bromide salt and 2-hydroxy-5-nitrobenzaldehyde in the presence of piperidine. 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and FTIR spectra of the Bromide salt and SP are shown and explained in the supporting file (Figs. S1-S6).

GQDs were prepared through the bottom-up method, through adjusting the degree of carbonization of citric acid precursor (Scheme 1). The resulting GQDs were characterized using FTIR, UV–vis, and fluorescence analyses. Prior to modification of GQDs by SP, it is necessary to enhance its surface hydrophobicity. To do this, during carbonization of citric acid, benzylamine was added to convert some free carboxylic acid to the corresponding benzyl amides (Scheme 1). After that, the as-prepared GQDs-Amine was modified with photochromic molecules. The structure of GQDs and GQDs-Amine were characterized using 1H NMR, 13C NMR, UV–vis, FTIR, and fluorescence spectroscopies. Finally, the surface of GQDs-Amine was modified with SP to fabricate GQDs-Amine-SP as the photochromic and fluorescent compound.

According to the 1H NMR spectra of the GQDs-Amine (Fig. 1 ), hydrogens that are farther from the surface of GQDs are usually detected by the NMR instrument. According to the 1H NMR spectra of GQDs and GQDs-Amine, a broad peak located in the region of 7.1–7.5 ppm is related to the phenyl ring of benzylamine (Ha-He). Also, the peak associated with the CH2 aliphatic (HN) of the benzylamine is observed as a doublet peak at 4.6 ppm. The peaks related to benzylamine and SP were observed in the 1H NMR spectra of GQDs-Amine-SP which confirms its successful synthesis (Fig. 1).

To evaluate the structure of the synthesized of GQDs, GQDs-Amine, and GQDs-Amine-SP, FT-IR spectroscopy was employed. As shown in Fig. S8, for the fluorescent GQDs, the peaks at 3432, 2871–2933 cm−1 are respectively due to the stretching vibrations of OH and CH. While the asymmetric and symmetric stretching frequenciesof COO- are appeared at 1562 and 1403 cm−1. In the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-Amine (Fig. S8), the new sharp peak at 1700 cm−1 is related to the amide bond formation between benzylamine and carboxylic acid of GQDs, while the intensity of peaks at 1562 and 1403 cm−1 related to COO− have decreased, representing that the successful functionalization of GQDs with benzylamine. Moreover, the broad peak of hydroxyl groups in the GQDs showed a significant reduction in the GQDs-Amine. Meanwhile, for the GQDs-Amine-SP (Fig. 4 ), the COCstretchesof theetherfunctionalgroup at 1272, 1160, and 1024 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of the nitro group at 1334 cm−1 were observed indicating the presence of spiropyran. [47] Other peaks related to the GQDs-Amine are in their original position.

Thermal stability of GQDs and GQDs-Amine-SP were evaluated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) analyses (Fig. S9). The first weight loss below 200 °C can be attributed to the removal ofthe adsorbed humidity. In the TGA diagram of GQDs, the second weight loss above 200 °C is due to theloss of the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs. In GQDs-Amine-SP, peaks at 258 and 390 °C are related to the degradation of SP molecules and functional groups containing oxygen atoms on the GQDs, respectively. Comparison of the TGA diagrams of the two materials shows that the thermal stability did not change after SP grafting onto the surface of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical behavior of GQDs and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 5 a, the solution of GQDs in dichloromethane shows a broad peak between 219 and 260 nm (transitions of π to π*) and a weak peak above 260 nm (transition of n to π*) in the absorption spectrum. [21] The solution of GQDs-Amine in dichloromethane also showed a similar pattern. Before UV irradiation, SP and GQDs-Amine-SP showed a definite absorption at 333 nm due to the SP which graduallyredshiftsto 341 and 355 nm after UV irradiation (365 nm) for 1 and 2 min, respectively (Fig. 5b). Also, after UV exposure a new absorption peak at 574 nm appeared due to the transformation of thering-closedSP to thering-opened merocyanine(MC) (Fig. 5b, Fig. S10). [48–49] The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs-Amine-SP confirms that the surface of GQDs was successfully functionalized with benzylamine and spiropyran and the synthesized photochromic compounds also have good optical properties.

When the excitation wavelength was changed from 300 to 450 nm, there was a shift of the maximum emission wavelength of the GQDs and GQDs-Amine, indicating their non-uniform particle size (Fig. 6 a). The excitation wavelength of 300 nm was selected due to the maximum emission at 419 nm (Fig. 6a and b). Also, after functionalization of GQDs with benzylamine, optical properties changed compared with GQDs. The emission spectra and the absorption spectra showed the redshift the blueshift, respectively. [47].

According to Fig. 7 , with increasing the UV exposure time (365 nm), the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs-Amine species at 476 nm steadily decreases and a new fluorescence emission peak appears at 627 nm, which progressively increases with increasing the UV irradiation time. This new emission peak is due to the isomeric form of MC. In addition, when GQDs-Amine-SP were irradiated under visible light (565 nm, 20 W LED), the FL intensity decreased at 627 nm and inversely the FL intensity gradually increased at 476 nm (Fig. 7b). Also, the maximum fluorescence emission peak of GQDs-Amine-SP gradually blue-shifted with increasing UV irradiation time, and red-shifted with visible light, because the fluorescence emission band of the MC overlaps with the emission band of GQDs-Amine, leading to blue and red shifts with an increase and decrease in energy transfer. The maximum FL intensity at 627 or 633 nm in the water–ethanol mixture is higher than dichloromethane due to further stabilization of the MC in polar solvents. [47] Also, the increase in FL peak intensity at 627 or 633 nm after UV light irradiation in polar solvents is much more significant than this increase in non-polar solvents such as dichloromethane (Fig. 7c and d). Fluorescence spectra of the pure SP in dichloromethane under exposureto theUVlight (365 nm) or visible light (565 nm) are shown in Fig. S11.

Effect of temperature on the thermal stability of the photogenerated MC isomer in water–ethanol was investigated. Checking the thermal stability of MC isomer in dichloromethane solvent was not possible due to its low boiling point of about 40 °C. According to the UV–vis spectra of SP in water–ethanol solvent, with increasing the temperature, the peak intensity and the λmax did not change, which shows the appropriate thermal stability of the photogenerated MC isomer in water–ethanol solvent (Fig. S12). When the polarity of the solvent increases, the maximum absorption of MC shifts to lower wavelengths ( λ = 563 and 540 nm for DCM and water–ethanol mixture respectively). With increasing the polarity of the solvent, the maximum absorption changes to lower wavelengths (higher frequency) and with decreasing polarity, the maximum absorption changes to higher wavelengths (lower frequency). Fluorescence spectra of the SP were also studied at different temperatures in water–ethanol solvent. With increasing the temperature, the emission intensity at wavelength of 630 nm related to the MC isomer increased from 25 to 60 °C, but higher increasing the temperature led to decrease the emission intensity due to the unstability of MC isomer at very high temperature (Fig. S12).

After UV irradiation, SP can be converted to MC, then energy emitted from the fluorescence of GQDs-Amine is transferred to the MC due to the suitable overlap between them (Scheme 2). Thus, the FL emission of GQDs-Amine is reduced by the MC species, and the color of GQDs-Amine can change from blue-green to red under UV exposure (365 nm). But under visible light, the MC converts to the closed-ring state SP and the energy transfer from GQDs-Amine to SP is inhibited because the energy of the GQDs-Amine and the SP cannot overlap together in this hybrid system. Also, fluorescence emission at 471 nm regarding GQDs-Amine can be recovered and GQDs-Amine-SP irradiated with visible light can show blue-green fluorescence again.

The photo-responsivity of the designed system is shown in Fig. 9 . In the GQDs-Amine-SP material under UV light irradiation for 220 s, the FL intensity at 471 nm decreased which is due to the conversion of the SP to the MC and transfer of the emitted energy to the MC (Fig. 9a). Whereas, under visible light irradiation (565 nm) the FL intensity gradually returned to its original value within 14 s due to thetransformationofMCinto theSP (Fig. 9c). In non-polar solvents such as dichloromethane, the MC is less stable and the conversion of MC to the SP occurs faster. As shown in Fig. 9b, in polar solvents, the fluorescence intensity at 476 nm gradually decreases after UV irradiation for 480 s, while with visible light irradiation, the fluorescence intensity at 476 nm gradually improves within 10 s. According to Fig. 9a and b, the rate of fluorescence switching in polar solvents such as water–ethanol mixture is higher than non-polar solvents. [47] The intrinsic rate constant of SP-MC reversible transformations is very large and the half-life of this compound is usually less than the picosecond time scale. [48].

Reversible fluorescence intensity switching of the GQDs-Amine-SP for at least ten cycles was investigated with alternate UV and visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 9e, after ten cycles, the fluorescence intensity was maintained and the reduction in the fatigue was not observed indicating the GQDs-Amine-SP is stable. Also, due to its reversible photoluminescence changes, it can be used as a light-controlled switch.

To confirm the FRET phenomena in the GQDs-Amine-SP, the physical mixture of GQDs-Amine and SP was prepared. The fluorescence spectra of the composition in dichloromethane and water–ethanol mixture solvent were recorded (Fig. S13). The physical mixture of GQDs-Amine and SP did not show any change in the fluorescence spectrum and intensity under UV light irradiation. Slight changes are due to the conversion of SP to MC, and the high fluorescence intensity of GQDs-Amine does not allow these changes to be well observed. As a result, the reversible fluorescence modulation of GQDs-Amine-SP can be attributed to FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the transparent photo-responsive films
ce:section.ce:para: In the second part of the study, transparent photo-responsive films consisting GQDs-Amine-SP compounds were fabricated to qualitative detection of ultraviolet (UV) lights present in the atmosphere. To study the effect of GQDs-Amine-SP on the surface tension of the prepared films, contact angle of the fabricated films with and without Amine-GQD-SP was measured. A water drop placed on the surface of the films and the contact angle before and after UV irradiation (365 nm) for 2 min was measured for each film (Fig. 10 b, c, e, and f). Due to the conversion of the SP to the MC with high polarity after UV irradiation, the wettability of the films containing GQDs-Amine-SP increased and contact angle decreased (Table S1). The most apparent manifestation of polarity switching is the level of change in wetting properties. Fig. 10 demonstrates this phenomenon for water droplets placed on PMMA/Amine-GQD-SP film. As shown in Fig. 10d, e, and f, the contact angle for PLA-based films was significantly reduced compared to PMMA-based films, due to the more hydrophilic property of PLA compared with PMMA.

The optical properties of the fabricated films were investigated using solid-state UV–vis spectroscopy, and a UV lamp at wavelength of 365 nm was employed to study the absorption and visual changes of these compounds. As shown in Fig. 11 , the absorption intensity increases with increasing the time of UV exposure due to increasing the conversion of SP molecules to the MC. Based on the images recorded from the polymeric films after UV exposure, the appeared color is consistently distributed with considerable intensity throughout the film. Moreover, the MC is more stable in the PLA/GQDs-Amine-SP film compared with PMMA/GQDs-Amine-SP film due to the hydrophilic property of PLA, which causes the higher color durability and intensity.

To investigate the possibility of FRET phenomena in the fabricated films, two films containing GQDs-Amine and SP were prepared separately. For better overlap between the fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs-Amine and the UV absorption spectra of the MC of the SP, the fluorochrome GQDs-Amine was excited at wavelength of 405 nm. According to Fig. S14, energy transfer from GQDs-Amine (donors) to MC (acceptor) is possible due to the appropriate overlap between donor and acceptor. After confirming the FRET phenomenon, fluorescence spectra of the polymeric films containing GQDs-Amine-SP were investigated. Figs. S15a and b show that the absorption intensity at 663 nm significantly increases with increasing the time of UV exposure, indicating the FRET occurs from the GQDs-Amine as donor to the MC as acceptor in the polymeric films. As shown in Fig. S16, at the same content of SP in both films, the PMMA/GQDs-Amine-SP film shows a greater fluorescence intensity than the PMMA/SP film (Fig. S16) which confirms that the fluorescence changes are not only due to the SP-MC conversions but also the energy transitions from the GQDs-Amine to MC. Also, solid-state fluorescence spectra are slightly wider, probably due to different phenomena such as intersystem transitions, internal and external transformations. Images of the photochromic films after sunlight exposure, obviously show the color changes (Fig. S17). So, the prepared films with photochromic and fluorescence properties can be used for UV-detecting, microscopic imaging, and holography.

In summary, transparent polymeric films containing GQDs-Amine-SP were developed as a new fluorescent sensor with FRET function by dispersion of GQDs-Amine-SP into the PLA and PMMA matrix. Prior to film preparation, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were made hydrophobic through the in situ amidation reaction of GQDs with benzylamine. Then the synthesized GQDs-Amine were decorated with spiropyran (SP). In the GQDs-Amine-SP, GQDs-Amine act as the fluorescent donor and spiropyran (SP) act as the photochromic acceptor. GQDs-Amine-SP showed good optical stability, reversibility, and fatigue resistance. GQDs-Amine-SP successfully demonstrated the FRET phenomenon. Different solutions of GQDs-Amine-SP in dichloromethane and ethanol/water mixture irradiated with UV light(365 nm) at various time intervals and their fluorescenceproperties were investigated under an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. With increasing the time of UV light exposure up to 220 s, the fluorescence intensity in 620 nm increased, and inversely, the fluorescence intensity in 471 nm decreased. GQDs-Amine-SP was homogenously dispersed in the PLA and PMMA matrix and transparent photochromic films were fabricated through phase inversion method and employed for UV-detection. Films indicated that the FRET occurs from the GQDs-Amine as donor to the MC as acceptor. The polymer films consisting of GQDs-Amine-SP compounds can also be used in digital holographic microscopy, etc.

Masoumeh Razaghi: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mojtaba Khorasani: Supervision. Zahra Mohamadnia: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Foad Kazemi: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114420.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
